elements.sent-2411-001: The 6th International Symposium on Space Sailing (ISSS 2023) took place on June 5-9, 2023 at the New York City College of Technology, the City University of New York.
elements.sent-2411-002: Since its inauguration in Herrsching (Germany, 2007), the ISSS has been held in New York (USA, 2010), Glasgow (UK, 2013), Kyoto (Japan, 2017) and Aachen (Germany, 2019).
elements.sent-2411-003: During the five-day symposium, participants from 14 countries gathered to discuss recent advances in space sailing, investigating new concepts and designs, describing innovative hardware and enabling technologies, strategies for dynamics and control, and providing updates on testing results for systems under development and future mission applications.
elements.sent-2411-004: As part of the 18 sessions, almost 50 oral presentations were held and, subsequently, 17 papers were submitted for review and publication.
elements.sent-2411-005: This paper aims to give an overview of all the cutting-edge technologies, detailed analysis and promising results shared with the scientific community as part of the event.
elements.sent-2411-006: Following the noteworthy deployment of the world’s first solar sail IKAROS in 2010, missions like NanoSail-D2 (2011) and LightSail-2 (2019) have showcased the potential of solar sailing technology through successful demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-007: Besides highlighting advancements in present and future programs, the symposium was an opportunity to reflect on objectives, design and test results from research centers and universities, as well as illustrate applications for interstellar travel, evaluate degrading performance and suggest alternative solutions for known limitations.
elements.sent-2411-008: The following Symposium is scheduled for early summer 2025 and will be hosted by TU Delft.
elements.sent-2411-009: Solar sails, first theorized by K.. Tsiolkovsky and F.
elements.sent-2411-010: Tsander back in the 1920s, are large sheets of low-areal density material whose only source of energy is the Sun electromagnetic flux \cite{tsander1969scientific .}
elements.sent-2411-011: At least in theory, a solar sail mission could be of unlimited duration, thanks to the “ever-present gentle push of sunlight” \cite{Vulpetti2008 ; a remarkable advantage is that no propellant is needed.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Scientists and writers have been dreaming of solar sailing for a long time; interest in solar sails grew in the 1970s with NASA's theoretical studies, though no practical missions materialized at the time.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the following decades, hundreds of theoretical and experimental studies on solar sailing were published.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, researchers in this field lacked regular opportunities to meet and collaborate.
elements.sent-2411-015: This led to the establishment of the 1st International Symposium on Solar Sailing (ISSS), which took place in June 2007 in Herrsching, Germany.
elements.sent-2411-016: The event focused on recent developments in solar sailing technologies and brought together the leading experts in the field.
elements.sent-2411-017: Three years later, in 2010, the 2nd ISSS was held in New York City, USA, where the JAXA team announced the successful deployment of IKAROS, the world’s first interplanetary solar sail (~200 m²), demonstrating spacecraft propulsion using solar radiation pressure (SRP).
elements.sent-2411-018: Advances in solar sail concepts, technologies, dynamics and control, and mission design were later documented in a special issue of Advances in Space Research (ASR) titled "Solar Sailing: Concepts, Technology, and Missions" \cite{KEZERASHVILI20111683 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Ten years later, after three more symposia (2013, 2017 and 2019 held in the UK, Japan, and Germany, respectively) and numerous publications in the field, a second special ASR issue reflected the vibrant reality of solar sailing research \cite{KEZERASHVILI20212559 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Meanwhile, review papers were also produced regularly on the topic \cite{Fu2016, Gong2019, ZHAO2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The main goal of this work is to provide an update on recent advances that have been presented at the latest symposium, highlighting achievements and results but also challenges that the community is trying to address.
elements.sent-2411-022: The 6th International Symposium on Space Sailing (ISSS 2023) extended from solar sailing to space sailing and covered a wide range of topics on sails' technology, with an emphasis on innovative mission concepts, materials and testing, control techniques, and mission design.
elements.sent-2411-023: These papers collectively push the boundaries of what is considered feasible with solar (but also magnetic and electric) sails, offering insights into both theoretical innovations and practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-024: Key contributions include the development of advanced mission trajectories for deep space and interstellar exploration, as well as robust control algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-025: Several papers focused on materials research to enhance solar sail durability, while others examined novel control mechanisms for managing momentum and attitude during long-duration missions.
elements.sent-2411-026: The interdisciplinary nature of this research shows how sails are becoming an increasingly viable technology for diverse space exploration missions.
elements.sent-2411-027: The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 gives an overview of propellantless propulsion systems for space exploration.
elements.sent-2411-028: Section 3 summarizes all achievements is solar sailing until now.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Section 4 details on symposium papers are shared, and conclusions are given in Section 5.
elements.sent-2411-030: Propellantless propulsion systems in space leverage external forces or mechanisms, eliminating the need for spacecraft to carry conventional chemical or electric propellant onboard.
elements.sent-2411-031: An example of a propellantless maneuver is the well-known gravity assist (or "fly-by"), using the pull of celestial bodies to change a spacecraft’s speed and velocity direction.
elements.sent-2411-032: This technique, employed in missions such as Voyager and Cassini \cite{longuski1990automated, cassini , requires the spacecraft to follow specific trajectories and is analogous to a finite burn, despite not requiring propellant.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Other environmental effects can also be used to propel a spacecraft.
elements.sent-2411-034: The primary advantage of these systems is the potential for virtually unlimited mission duration, as they are not constrained by fuel reserves, thus removing the concern of depleting propulsion resources during the mission.
elements.sent-2411-035: This makes them particularly attractive for long-duration space exploration.
elements.sent-2411-036: The Sun serves as the primary energy source within the Solar System, emitting both electromagnetic radiation and charged particles.
elements.sent-2411-037: Electromagnetic radiation spans the entire spectrum, including ultraviolet (UV), visible, and infrared (IR) light, and carries momentum that can be transferred to objects upon reflection or absorption — this is the principle behind solar sails.
elements.sent-2411-038: Solar sails use the momentum transfer from photons to accelerate spacecraft \cite{mcinnes1999solar .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Electromagnetic radiation hits the highly reflective surface of the sail, it imparts a small force that continuously pushes the craft forward.
elements.sent-2411-040: Over time, this force accumulates, enabling the spacecraft to reach high velocities.
elements.sent-2411-041: Additionally, the Sun produces the solar wind, a stream of charged particles (mainly protons and electrons) that continuously flows outward.
elements.sent-2411-042: Magnetic sails and electric sails harness the solar wind to generate thrust.
elements.sent-2411-043: Magnetic Sails ("Mag-sails") utilize a large loop of superconducting wire to create a magnetic field that interacts with the solar wind \cite{zubrin1991magnetic, zubrin1991advanced .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Electric sails ("E-sails"), instead, employ long charged tethers that repel solar wind protons (more advantageous than electrons due to their higher mass), to generate thrust \cite{janhunen2004electric, janhunen2010electric .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Similar to solar sails but powered by lasers, photon sails use directed energy (from lasers) to accelerate the sail \cite{marx1966interstellar,Lubin2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The idea is to build ground-based or space-based laser arrays that emit photons directly onto the sail.
elements.sent-2411-047: This concept is central to the Breakthrough Starshot initiative, which aims to send probes to nearby stars like Alpha Centauri \cite{Breakthrough24 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Some research has also been done on trying to enhance also other radiation sources from stars, artificial sources, or other celestial phenomena \cite{forward1984photon, matloff1988interstellar .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Besides large area structures like sails, a few other solutions have been proposed, such as electrodynamic tethers, that would interact with the magnetic field \cite{sanmartin1993bare, hoyt2001terminator, landis2007lorentz, forward1976propulsion, TethersLes20 , however, this technology would be limited in its application by the presence and intensity of the field.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Each of these methods avoids the need for onboard propellants, relying instead on external forces like sunlight, solar wind, magnetic fields, or gravity.
elements.sent-2411-051: When examining forces within the Solar System, solar radiation is significantly more influential in deep space than others.
elements.sent-2411-052: Table \ref{tab:envF illustrates that both at 1 AU and 5 AU (near Earth and Jupiter, respectively), the effects of solar wind, drag, and magnetic fields are comparatively less dominant \cite{Longuski92, Fu2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The provided values correspond to distances of 10 planetary radii from the respective planets.
elements.sent-2411-054: This explains why solar sails have always been considered the most promising propellantless propulsion mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-055: Nevertheless, researchers have continued to investigate alternatives and possibly hybrid solutions to meet different needs depending on the mission requirements.
elements.sent-2411-056: The ISSS has always welcomed novel approaches and is meant to bring together innovative perspectives.

elements.sent-2411-057: The first attempt to showcase solar sailing was the Cosmos-1 mission, which was a project by Cosmos Studios and The Planetary Society to test a solar sail in space.
elements.sent-2411-058: This mission failed due to a launch vehicle malfunction in 2005 \cite{Reichhardt2005 Japan's IKAROS mission, launched by JAXA in 2010 \cite{Tsuda2011, KEZERASHVILI20111683, Tsuda2013 , became the first spacecraft to successfully employ solar sail propulsion in interplanetary space.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The sail generated measurable thrust, proving that sunlight could effectively power spacecraft for deep space missions \cite{mcinnes1999solar .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In parallel, NASA’s NanoSail-D2 mission \cite{NanoD2011 , launched the same year, validated the viability of solar sailing in Earth orbit on a smaller scale.}
elements.sent-2411-061: These early milestones paved the way for more advanced applications.
elements.sent-2411-062: In 2015 LightSail-1 successfully deployed its sail \cite{Biddy2012 and in 2019 LightSail-2 became the first small spacecraft to change its orbit using only sunlight for propulsion \cite{planetary2019lightsail2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This success of the Planetary Society's sails highlighted their potential for propelling small, fuel-less spacecraft, critical for long-duration missions in deep space.
elements.sent-2411-064: Ongoing research continues to focus on improving sail materials and deployment mechanisms for future interplanetary missions.
elements.sent-2411-065: A recent milestone was achieved with GAMA’s first demonstrator mission, “GAMA Alpha”, launched aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 on January 3, 2023.
elements.sent-2411-066: The 6U CubeSat, weighing just 12 kg (about the size of a large shoebox), carried a 73 m$^2$ solar sail.
elements.sent-2411-067: The Gama Alpha mission focused on demonstrating successful sail deployment, while its follow-up mission, Gama Beta, will aim to demonstrate solar sail propulsion and navigation \cite{GAMAnews1 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: NanoAvionics, a Lithuanian company specializing in nanosatellite platforms, played a crucial role in the development of Gama Alpha, Europe’s first solar sail mission, designed with the support of the French Space Agency, CNES.
elements.sent-2411-069: NanoAvionics provided critical components for Gama Alpha, including electrical systems, telecommunications, and on-board hardware, which were essential in enabling the mission's success \cite{GAMAnews2, GAMAnews3 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Building on the success of the Gama mission, NanoAvionics also supplied the satellite bus for NASA’s Advanced Composite Solar Sail System (ACS3).
elements.sent-2411-071: ACS3 is a 12U CubeSat (measuring 23x23x34 cm and weighing 16 kg) carrying an 80 m$^2$ solar sail, designed to demonstrate solar sail technology for future small spacecraft applications \cite{Wilkie .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Among its objectives is to showcase solar sails' capabilities for orbit control, including adjusting the semimajor axis to achieve various orbital altitudes.
elements.sent-2411-073: At ISSS 2023, Andres Dono presented ACS3's flight dynamics system, which supports mission planning and solar sail trajectory modeling, and integrates with ground software for orbit determination and real-time analysis.
elements.sent-2411-074: Following its April launch, ACS3 was confirmed as fully operational on August 29, 2024 \cite{ACS3news .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Table \ref{tab:successful reports missions that have been successful since IKAROS launch in 2010, whereas failed attempts are listed in Table \ref{tab:failed .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: In all circumstances lessons learnt came out of the process: failure has to be considered an option when pushing the boundaries of technology.

elements.sent-2411-077: Several solar sail missions have faced significant challenges, resulting in either failure or cancellation.
elements.sent-2411-078: The Cosmos-1 mission, launched by The Planetary Society in 2005, was intended to be the first spacecraft to demonstrate solar sail propulsion.
elements.sent-2411-079: However, it failed due to a malfunction in the launch vehicle, preventing the spacecraft from reaching orbit.
elements.sent-2411-080: NASA's Solar Sail Demonstrator, initiated in 1999, was canceled before launch due to budgetary and technical constraints, stalling further solar sail research at that time.
elements.sent-2411-081: In 2008, NASA’s NanoSail-D mission aimed to test solar sail technology in low Earth orbit, but the Falcon-1 rocket failed, resulting in the loss of the spacecraft.
elements.sent-2411-082: Russia’s ROSCOSMOS also encountered setbacks with its Znamya 2.5 mission, wide space solar mirror, in 1999.
elements.sent-2411-083: It had a diameter of 25 m, and was expected to produce a bright spot 7 km in diameter, with luminosity between five and ten full moons.
elements.sent-2411-084: However, soon after deployment, the reflector caught on an antenna on the Progress and ripped.
elements.sent-2411-085: The reflector failed to deploy properly and broke apart.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth]{figures/Sunjammer.png} \caption{Sunjammer dimensions compared to other sails and the Space Shuttle. Credits O. Eldad and G. Lightsey \cite{SunjammerSmallSat}.} \label{fig:sunjammer}
elements.sent-2411-086: NASA's Sunjammer mission, led by the private company L’Garde Inc.
elements.sent-2411-087: Similarly, the European Space Agency's Gossamer mission was canceled in 2015 after facing technical issues and shifting priorities within ESA \cite{Gossamer11 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Sunjammer, named after a short story written by Sir Arthur C.
elements.sent-2411-089: Clarke, with a surface of 1200 m$^2$ (and a weight of just 45 kg) was meant to be much bigger than sails designed before, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:sunjammer .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Its area was twice that of Cosmos-1 (600 m$^2$) and around six times that of IKAROS (200 m$^2$).
elements.sent-2411-091: More recently, the Near-Earth Asteroid Scout (NEA Scout), a CubeSat mission launched aboard NASA's Artemis I in 2022, was expected to be the first CubeSat to study a near-Earth asteroid using solar sail propulsion.
elements.sent-2411-092: Unfortunately, after launch, communication was never established with the spacecraft, and multiple attempts to deploy its solar sail failed \cite{NEAScout20 .}
elements.sent-2411-093: The mission was subsequently declared lost, marking another setback in solar sail development.
elements.sent-2411-094: Another interesting concept proposal was the Solar Cruiser, with a surface of more than 1600 m$^2$, which was expected to launch as a rideshare payload alongside the Interstellar Mapping and Acceleration Probe (IMAP) in February 2025 \cite{SolCruiser21 .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Although the Solar Cruiser mission was not approved to advance to phase C, its closeout plan included the development and advancement of several key technologies as well as the demonstration of a full quadrant sail deployment, which was successfully completed \cite{SolarCruiserNASA and achieved NASA’s Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 6 through extensive testing (January 2024) \cite{IAC24_Johnson .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: It is clear that, despite these challenges, valuable lessons from the unsuccessful and canceled missions continue to inform and improve the design and operation of future solar sail projects.
elements.sent-2411-097: Fig. \ref{fig:w_A shows mass and area features for some of the missions mentioned above.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Their characteristics are also reported in Fig. \ref{fig:timeline and in Table \ref{tab:status_a_m ordered by their launch dates.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Although the basic idea behind solar sailing appears simple, challenging engineering problems have to be solved \cite{LEIPOLD2003, Challenges19 .}
elements.sent-2411-100: The Solar Sailing community is actively trying to address these by fostering collaboration and regularly sharing updates on progress in conferences and forums such as the ISSS.
elements.sent-2411-101: Ref. \cite{BERTHET2024101047 presents a recent review and future overlook on space sails for achieving major space exploration goals.}

elements.sent-2411-102: After providing a concise overview of major milestones in space sailing, this section reviews the research presented at ISSS 2023.
elements.sent-2411-103: This review aims to highlight key findings and promising applications, though it cannot fully capture the depth of topics addressed in the five-day symposium.
elements.sent-2411-104: For further details, readers can access the complete set of presentations and papers online \cite{ISSS23 .}
elements.sent-2411-105: At the forefront of innovative space sailing concepts, solutions covered various areas: mission design and analysis, advanced materials, attitude control strategies, and deployment methods.
elements.sent-2411-106: The symposium was not only a look toward future missions but also a chance to reflect on past milestones like those related to LightSail-2, ACS3, Gama Alpha and the Solar Cruiser.
elements.sent-2411-107: Multiple presentations referenced these missions, discussing their results and the implications for advancing solar sail research.
elements.sent-2411-108: The Symposium kicked off with key talks by NASA and JAXA on their latest missions.
elements.sent-2411-109: Les Johnson (NASA Marshall Space Flight Center) reported on the status of NASA’s Solar Cruiser Solar Sail System and its readiness for heliophysics and deep space missions.
elements.sent-2411-110: With a sailcraft platform using a 2.5-micron thick sail measuring 1,653 m$^2$ with pointing control and attitude stability comparable to traditional platforms, Solar Cruiser's demonstration mission aimed at maturing solar sail propulsion technology and enabling near-term, high-priority breakthrough science missions \cite{LesJ2022 .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/solarcruiser.png} \caption{The Solar Cruiser quadrant sail deployment and Solar Cruiser team.} \label{fig:team}
elements.sent-2411-111: Osamu Mori presented JAXA’s new Solar Power Sail Program, marking a continuation of Japan’s efforts in the Post-OKEANOS Era.
elements.sent-2411-112: OKEANOS (Oversize Kite-craft for Exploration and AstroNautics in the Outer Solar System) is a Japanese mission concept in which electric power is generated by thin-film solar cells integrated with the solar sail membrane \cite{OKEANOS .}
elements.sent-2411-113: The proposed solar sail-powered spacecraft was designed to rendezvous and return from a Trojan asteroid, making it currently the only solution for asteroid sample return.
elements.sent-2411-114: However, due to budget constraints, OKEANOS has not been selected for a JAXA mission.
elements.sent-2411-115: The new solar power sail program aims to expand the technology’s use in various missions by incorporating: i.
elements.sent-2411-116: Andrew Nutter (GAMA, French space company) presented the objectives, design, and initial test results of GAMA’s upcoming GAMA-Beta Solar Sail.
elements.sent-2411-117: GAMA’s staged approach to technology development involves a series of missions with increasingly complex sail configurations.
elements.sent-2411-118: Their first demonstrator mission, "GAMA Alpha," successfully deployed a solar sail controlled from a CubeSat, gathering flight data to improve simulations and control algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-119: After two years of rapid development, the Alpha satellite was launched on January 3, 2023, aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9.
elements.sent-2411-120: The next mission, "GAMA Beta," aims to demonstrate controlled navigation in a high Low Earth Orbit (LEO), achieving precise orbit adjustments using photonic pressure alone.
elements.sent-2411-121: Secondary objectives include qualifying systems for deep-space navigation.
elements.sent-2411-122: The electric solar wind sail (E-sail) \cite{janhunen2004electric, janhunen2010electric utilizes Coulomb drag propulsion, harnessing solar wind for interplanetary movement.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Pekka Janhunen presented the status of E-sail developments and mission designs.
elements.sent-2411-124: Designed for regions beyond Earth's magnetosphere, E-sails use centrifugal force to stretch their tethers.
elements.sent-2411-125: Thrust vectoring is achieved by adjusting the spin plane, and thrust magnitude can be controlled via current and voltage adjustments on the electron gun.
elements.sent-2411-126: For larger spacecraft, multi-tether E-sails are necessary, including auxiliary tethers connecting the main tether tips.
elements.sent-2411-127: In this configuration, differential voltage control allows for independent spin rate and plane control.
elements.sent-2411-128: Multi-tether E-sails can counteract secular spin rate changes due to Coriolis forces, unlike single-tether variants, which require auxiliary propulsion.
elements.sent-2411-129: Coulomb drag propulsion offers a promising approach for orbital debris management and free travel within the solar system.
elements.sent-2411-130: In its original form, an E-sail consists of a grid of charged tethers held at high positive voltage, interacting with solar wind ions to generate propulsion \cite{janhunen2010electric .}
elements.sent-2411-131: Given the difficulty of deploying large tether structures in deep space, recent work has focused on smaller satellite missions.
elements.sent-2411-132: Lorenzo Niccolai’s (Pisa University) study \cite{Niccolai2024 examines deep-space transfers for small spacecraft equipped with E-sails and electric thrusters.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Solar panels provide power to the electric thrusters, scaling with the inverse square of heliocentric distance.
elements.sent-2411-134: The thrust model \cite{Mengali2018 describes E-sail propulsion as a function of tether spin plane attitude and Sun-spacecraft distance, solving the optimal control problem using an indirect multiple shooting method based on Pontryagin’s maximum principle.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Many symposium talks covered new mission concepts and examined their feasibility with current or emerging technologies.
elements.sent-2411-136: Interplanetary missions aimed at reaching the heliopause, distant stars, and asteroids were presented.
elements.sent-2411-137: Observations from 2016 indicate the presence of a small, potentially habitable planet orbiting Proxima Centauri, sparking renewed interest in interstellar exploration \cite{TerrPlanet2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-138: At ISSS 2023, several talks examined how solar sails could be employed for interstellar missions.
elements.sent-2411-139: Bernd Dachwald (FH Aachen University of Applied Sciences, Germany) and his collaborators investigated photon-sail capture trajectories within the Alpha Centauri star system, focusing on minimum-time transfer and orbit-raising strategies in habitable zones \cite{Dachwald2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-140: Tim Rotmans (Delft University of Technology) studied trajectories from the binary star system towards planet Proxima b \cite{ISSS_Rotmans .}
elements.sent-2411-141: The classical Lagrange points in the binary system are used to find possible trajectories towards Proxima b and heteroclinic connections are exploited using a patched restricted three-body problem method to connect the two phases.
elements.sent-2411-142: Four futuristic sail configurations carrying a payload of 10 grams are used: two sails with a reflective coating on one side and two sails with a reflective coating on both sides.
elements.sent-2411-143: Rotmans work targets the Alpha Centauri system, the destination of the popular Breakthrough Starshot project \cite{Breakthrough24 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Rotmans and colleagues explored graphene-based sails, achieving lightness numbers up to 1779, which drastically reduce travel time.
elements.sent-2411-145: These studies represent a bold step toward interstellar missions powered by light alone, presenting novel trajectory designs that minimize time and energy usage.
elements.sent-2411-146: Greg Matloff (City University of New York) considered an application of Statite-type photon sail probes to achieve rectilinear trajectories to explore the outer solar system and near-interstellar destinations.
elements.sent-2411-147: Statute-type solar photon sails are sufficiently thin and reflective and solar radiation-pressure force on the sail exactly balances solar gravitational force.
elements.sent-2411-148: In such a force-free environment, the spacecraft exits the solar system at its pre-sail-deployment solar-orbital velocity.
elements.sent-2411-149: Here we consider departures from a circular 1-AU solar orbit, the perihelion of a 0.7-1 AU elliptical solar orbit, and the perihelion of a 0.3-a 1U solar orbit.
elements.sent-2411-150: Possible outer-solar-system destinations include Europa, Titan, Enceladus, Arrokoth and more distant such as the Sun’s Gravitational Focus.
elements.sent-2411-151: Fil's Project Svarog \cite{ISSS_Fil, Fil2023 discusses a novel mission concept using passively stabilized solar sails to propel spacecraft up to the heliopause.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The Svarog system could serve as a low-cost enabler for the testing of new technologies and research opportunities in deep space, piggybacking the increasing number of interplanetary missions, and fostering deep space exploration.
elements.sent-2411-153: Exploring the outer solar system using solar sail propulsion requires achieving high cruise speeds, which can be facilitated by accelerating the sailcraft in the region near the Sun.
elements.sent-2411-154: Deploying a solar sail close to the Sun maximizes the force exerted by the radiation, enabling higher acceleration and faster cruise speeds.
elements.sent-2411-155: Missions to the Kuiper Belt region and beyond, the Oort Cloud, the gravitational focus of the Sun, and even the Alpha-Centauri system \cite{Lubin2016 are considered the next breakthrough for space exploration.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Among various suggested destinations, the trans-Neptunian object Sedna (90377) has recently gained more and more interest from the scientific community \cite{Zubko2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Sedna, orbiting the Sun in a highly eccentric orbit, is currently on the way to its perihelion (around 76 AU from the Sun), and recent studies consider this an extraordinary opportunity to get to know more about deep space, being its aphelion at about 936 AU.
elements.sent-2411-158: Elena Ancona (Politecnico di Bari) presented the latest results on the ongoing research to reach Sedna with a solar sail \cite{IAC24_Ancona .}
elements.sent-2411-159: The authors recommend using a solar sail that takes advantage of the thermal desorption of its coating as an additional propulsion mechanism, besides its conventional acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-160: During the process of thermal desorption one can neglect the acceleration due to solar radiation because this acceleration is an order of magnitude less than the one due to thermal desorption \cite{Kezer2015, Ancona2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-161: It was considered a perihelion of 0.3 AU for and the desorption effects on the cruise speed of the solar sail.
elements.sent-2411-162: Application of this technology with state-of-the-art sails can result in solar-system exit velocities over 100 km/s, as mentioned in previous studies targeting Kuiper Belt Objects \cite{Ancona2019_KB .}
elements.sent-2411-163: The Mars-Jupiter asteroid belt remains one of the least explored regions of the solar system.
elements.sent-2411-164: The use of a solar sail for a long-term mission into the asteroid belt was considered in Bakhyt Alipova's presentation \cite{ISSS_Alipova .}

elements.sent-2411-228: Attitude control is a critical aspect of solar sail design, as the orientation of the sail directly affects its thrust direction.
elements.sent-2411-229: Conventional spacecraft attitude control methods, such as control moment gyros, reaction wheels, and thrusters, are generally unsuitable for solar sails, especially thrusters, since solar sails are intended to operate without propellant.
elements.sent-2411-230: Ideally, the sail’s center of mass ($c.m.$) aligns with its center of pressure ($c.p.$), but minor manufacturing and deployment errors can create an offset, causing unwanted torque that must be corrected for stable attitude.
elements.sent-2411-231: Conventional attitude control methods cannot effectively maintain the sail’s attitude for several reasons, as first discussed by Wie \cite{Wie2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-232: Fortunately, by having an attitude control strategy that allows a controlled $c.m.$-$c.p.$ offset, desired attitude torques can be generated.
elements.sent-2411-233: Fu et al. \cite{Fu2016 give a comprehensive overview of the most common methods for attitude control, for both rigid sailcraft such as control vane, gimbaled/sliding masses, shifted/tilted wings and non-rigid ones.}
elements.sent-2411-234: Non-rigid sailcraft rely on internal tension from centrifugal forces and cannot use mechanisms that apply large out-of-plane loads.
elements.sent-2411-235: For non-rigid sails, an attitude control method using reflectivity-controlled membranes has been developed: by adjusting voltage, the membrane's reflectivity changes, shifting the cp and generating a torque for attitude control.
elements.sent-2411-236: Precise control is essential for the success of solar sail missions.

elements.sent-2411-297: By coating the solar sail with materials that undergo desorption at elevated temperatures, one can introduce an additional acceleration mechanism \cite{Ancona2019, Ancona2019_KB .}
elements.sent-2411-298: As the sail heats up from solar radiation at a specific heliocentric distance, the coated material desorbs, releasing additional energy that boosts the sail's speed \cite{Kezer2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-299: This effect, combined with the traditional solar radiation pressure, provides an extra source of propulsion.
elements.sent-2411-300: The perihelion of the solar sail's orbit is selected based on the temperature required for the desorption of the coating material.
elements.sent-2411-301: At this critical perihelion, the sail, that was stowed, is deployed and reaches its maximum temperature, triggering desorption and subsequent acceleration.

elements.sent-2411-336: New concepts and mission designs, innovative hardware and enabling technologies, as well as strategies for dynamics and control, were presented to the public during the 6th International Symposium on Space Sailing held in 2023 at the CUNY New York City College of Technology \cite{ISSS23 .}
elements.sent-2411-337: The ISSS 2023 offered a rich platform for collaboration, knowledge-sharing, and exploration of the latest developments in solar sailing technology.
elements.sent-2411-338: The symposium underscored the progress made since early missions like IKAROS and LightSail-2, showcasing how advancements in materials, control strategies, and mission design are steadily addressing the engineering challenges of propellant-less propulsion.
elements.sent-2411-339: Recent missions such as NASA’s ACS3 and Solar Cruiser, GAMA-Beta Solar Sail, and post-OKEANOS development were also explored, providing valuable insights into the performance and potential applications of solar sail technology.
elements.sent-2411-340: These projects, along with other innovative research presented, are pivotal in realizing future interstellar and deep space exploration goals.
elements.sent-2411-341: With a strong emphasis on continued innovation and collaboration, the symposium highlighted the importance of evolving this field through international cooperation and shared insights.
elements.sent-2411-342: As the community prepares for the next ISSS at TU Delft in 2025, the discussions and findings from ISSS 2023 will undoubtedly be helpful in the definition of new space exploration concepts.
elements.sent-2411-343: Solar sails use the momentum transfer from photons to accelerate spacecraft \cite{Polyakhova,mcinnes1999solar, MatloffBook2005,Vulpetti2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-344: Comprehensive reviews of the magnetic and electric sails and their application can be found in Refs.. \cite{janhunen2010electric,BassettoEL2021,Djojodihardjo2018, BassettoMag2022 .}
